title: Ion-polymer interactions in gel electrolytes studied by ESR spectroscopy of spin probes and spin labels
abstract:

Electrolytes Studied by
Spectroscopy
ESR
of Spin Probes and Spin Labels

2 I.U.T. Belfort-Montbe liard, BP 427, 25211 Montbe liard Cedex, France

Abstract: Ion-conducting gels containing poly(methyl methacrylate), propylene
carbonate and lithium perchlorate have been studied by ESR spectroscopy using
spin-probe and spin-label techniques. Trends in
with increasing salt con-

centration observed with di†erent polymer contents are indicative of strong
cationÈmatrix interactions which may restrict cation mobility in these systems.

Polym. Int. 47, 16È19 (1998)
Key words: polymer electrolytes; ESR spectroscopy; spin probes; poly(methyl
methacrylate); ionic conductivity; viscosity
INTRODUCTION
Polymer gel electrolytes are increasingly being investi-
gated for use in electrochromic devices,1,2 primary3 and
secondary
batteries,4
and
electrochemical
super-
capacitors.5,6
High
ionic
conductivities
(about
10~3 S cm~1) are achieved by immobilizing a solution
of salt in organic solvent (e.g.
in propylene
LiClO4
carbonate) in an appropriate polymer matrix, e.g.
poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA).
The fundamental characteristics of such gels are illus-
trated in Fig. 1 which is taken from data in recent
papers by Bohnke et al.7 The addition of polymer (in
this case amorphous PMMA) leads to massive increases
in viscosity, but to only modest decreases in conductiv-
ity.
Put very simply, it is possible to regard these gels
either as biphasic systems where the ions move exclu-
sively in the organic solvent and their mobility is
restricted by percolation e†ects, or as single-phase
systems where the ions are coordinated by both the
* Author to whom all correspondence should be addressed.

Contract/grant number: GR/H94290.
polymer and the solvent. At present, there is no clear
indication which is the better of these two descriptions.
Bohnke et al.7 examined the variations in conductiv-
ity (p), viscosity (g) and glass transition temperature Tg
in a wide range of
gels. They con-

tant in these systems. However, Cazzanelli et al.8 were
unable to Ðnd evidence for such interactions by Raman

We report on the use of spin probes and spin labels,
as described elsewhere by Cameron and co-workers,
9h11 to probe microscopic viscosities in these gels by
ESR spectroscopy. Trends in
obtained for the

4-oxo-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl
(4-oxo-
TEMPO) probe and for end-labelled PMMA (see struc-
tures in Fig. 2) dissolved in the gels should indicate
changes in structural dynamics which are informative
with respect to both ionÈsolvent and ionÈpolymer inter-
actions.
EXPERIMENTAL
A
1000 ppm
stock
solution
of
4-oxo-TEMPO
in
dichloromethane was prepared. Small quantities were
added to a solution of
PMMA
in
(Mn \ 62 900)
dichloromethane, which was then mixed with the
appropriate amount of a known solution of
in
LiClO4
propylene carbonate (PC). The dichloromethane was
removed by rotary evaporation at 40¡C for several
hours. Two series of gels were prepared in which the
PMMA contents were 25 and 45 wt%, respectively, and
the
concentrations (with respect to PC) were
LiClO4
varied from 0 to 1É5 M. For the end-labelled experi-
ments, the gels were dosed with comparable amounts of
labelled PMMA (Mn \ 27 300).
ESR data were collected by a Bruker ECS106
spectrometer with built-in variable temperature control
unit (Eurotherm B-VT2000). Gels were transferred to
the ESR tube and saturated with argon to eliminate
traces of oxygen. During each experiment, the sample
underwent a heating sweep from 150 to 290 K, with
spectra recorded at 10 K intervals.

It may be noted that this “top valueÏ of

The Ðrst point to note is that the oxo-TEMPO probe
signals increases in microscopic viscosity (plots 1 and 2
in Fig. 5) which parallel the increases in shear viscosity
measured by conventional rheometry. Thus, Bohnke et
increases in shear viscosity in the 45 wt% than in the
The question now is whether this rise in viscosity
depends on ionÈPC or ionÈPMMA interactions. The
answer will depend on the behaviour of the end-labelled
polymers. There is need for caution here because any
for the label could indicate sti†ening of
the polymer chains either because of ionÈpolymer inter-
actions or because the polymer is being “salted outÏ
owing to strong ionÈsolvent interactions.
It is worth, therefore, considering in a little more
detail what salting out implies. This is an old termino-
logy, which encompasses the (sometimes large) increases
in activity coefficients of charged and neutral species in
relatively concentrated ionic solutions (see for example,
Fig. 6. Schematic diagram showing interaction of Li` ions
with PMMA chains and action of
ref. 12). This e†ect arises when there is insufficient
solvent to satisfy the solvation requirements of ions and
other solutes present (in this case the polymer).
Returning to the discussion of Fig. 5, one sees that
for the end-labels in the polymer-rich gel (plot 4)
rises even for small additions of
words, what we see is a dilute solution e†ect. This is not
really suggestive of salting out, but is consistent with the
argument put forward by Bohnke et al.7 that the Li`
ions are interacting strongly with the polymer in these
PMMA-rich gels. One mechanism for the chain sti†en-
ing e†ect is shown schematically in Fig. 6. The Li` ions
are coordinated by carbonyl oxygens on the ester side
transient crosslinks (see also ref. 13).
The behaviour of end-labels in the polymer-poor gel
(plot 3) reveals an additional degree of complexity. The
is rather small, which is consistent
viscosity7 mentioned above, but
higher concentrations. It is tempting to attribute this
to the salting-out e†ect. It is worth
remarking that it is in the same concentration range
that the oxo-TEMPO probe also shows an increase in
Although these ESR probes reveal a remarkable
wealth of information about ionÈsolvent and ionÈ
polymer interactions in gels, they cannot themselves
shed light on the mechanism of ion transport. To deÐne
more closely the transport mechanism, one must have
information concerning ionic transference numbers. It is
certainly possible that the apparent “decouplingÏ of ion
mobility from the shear viscosity, which is implied in
move about, while the Li` ions are coordinated to the
polymer. The question of which ions are moving in gels
needs to be addressed. However, the measurement of
reliable transference numbers in polymer electrolytes is
a far from simple problem.16h18
Just how much the ionÈpolymer interaction identiÐed
above inÑuences the cation and anion mobilities is diffi-
cult to judge. It is interesting to note that gels have been
prepared where ionÈmatrix interactions are deliberately
minimized.14,15 It would be informative, therefore, to
compare the results of spin probe and also transference
number experiments over a wider range of polymer gel
The ESR spectroscopy of spin-probed and spin-labelled
gels reveals a surprisingly complex
pattern of interactions in these systems. The original
idea that these gels are simply organic electrolytes con-
strained within a polymer matrix seems too simple.
ESR study of ionÈpolymer interactions in gel electrolytes
Instead, it looks as if the cations (in this case Li`) inter-
act directly with the polymer, and that these inter-
actions are largely responsible for the increases in
viscosity observed upon addition of salt. The mecha-
nism of ion transport in gels may turn out therefore to
be not too di†erent from that in those polyether electro-
lytes plasticized with propylene carbonate which have
